annotations.label: Likelihood-ratio test

anchor.snippet: the likelihood-ratio test is a hypothesis test that involves comparing the goodness of fit of two competing statistical models

annotations.note: Mathlib has no hypothesis-testing framework; only the log-likelihood ratio function `llr` between measures is defined, not a test procedure.

annotations.label: Likelihood ratio test statistic (general)

anchor.snippet: The likelihood ratio test statistic for the null hypothesis

annotations.note: No notion of supremum of a likelihood function over a parameter subspace as a test statistic is present in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Bounds on the likelihood ratio

anchor.snippet: the likelihood ratio is bounded between zero and one and the likelihood ratio test statistic between 0 and infinity

annotations.note: No declaration in Mathlib establishes the 0–1 bounds of a likelihood ratio test statistic since the statistic itself is not formalized.

annotations.label: Log-likelihood form of the test statistic

anchor.snippet: Often the likelihood-ratio test statistic is expressed as a difference between the log-likelihoods

annotations.note: Mathlib defines `llr μ ν x := log (μ.rnDeriv ν x).toReal`, capturing the log-likelihood ratio between measures, but not the supremum-based test-statistic form used in hypothesis testing.

annotations.label: Nested models requirement

anchor.snippet: The likelihood-ratio test requires that the models be nested

annotations.note: No notion of nested statistical models or parameter-subspace containment for testing is formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Simple-vs.-simple likelihood ratio

anchor.snippet: A simple-vs.-simple hypothesis test has completely specified models under both the null hypothesis and the alternative hypothesis

annotations.note: No formalization of simple null/alternative hypotheses or the corresponding likelihood ratio framework in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Decision rule for likelihood-ratio test

anchor.snippet: The likelihood-ratio test provides the decision rule as follows

annotations.note: Mathlib contains no formalization of test critical regions, rejection rules, or decision procedures.

annotations.label: Neyman–Pearson lemma

anchor.snippet: The Neyman–Pearson lemma states that this likelihood-ratio test is the most powerful among all level

annotations.note: Search for `Neyman`/`Pearson`/`MostPowerful` in Mathlib returns no results; the Neyman–Pearson lemma is not formalized.

annotations.label: Interpretation of low/high likelihood-ratio values

anchor.snippet: Low values of the likelihood ratio mean that the observed result was much less likely to occur under the null hypothesis as compared to the alternative

annotations.note: This is an informal interpretive remark about a non-formalized object and has no counterpart in Mathlib.

annotations.label: One-sample t-test from likelihood ratio

anchor.snippet: Suppose that we have a random sample, of size n , from a population that is normally-distributed

annotations.note: No t-test, Student-t distribution, or worked likelihood-ratio derivation thereof is present in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Wilks' theorem

anchor.snippet: Assuming H 0 is true, there is a fundamental result by Samuel S. Wilks

annotations.note: Grep finds no `Wilks` declaration or chi-squared distribution in Mathlib, so the asymptotic χ²-distribution result is unformalized.
